exercises.title: @Value vs @ConfigurationProperties — which is better?
exercises.scenario: Choose the right binding approach for each situation.
exercises.instructions:
For each scenario, choose @Value or @ConfigurationProperties and justify:

A. One property: `server.cors.allowed-origin=https://app.example.com`
B. Five related properties: `payment.gateway.url`, `.timeout`, `.retries`, `.api-key`, `.currency`
C. Properties needed in 3 different service classes
D. Properties that must be validated at startup (e.g. URL must use HTTPS)
E. A quick one-off property for a test
exercises.expected_solution:
A → @Value (single property, no grouping benefit)
B → @ConfigurationProperties (5 related properties, one prefix)
C → @ConfigurationProperties (inject one record everywhere)
D → @ConfigurationProperties + @Validated (fail-fast validation)
E → @Value (quick, test-only)

test_cases.expected:
A → @Value (single property, no grouping benefit)
B → @ConfigurationProperties (5 related properties, one prefix)
C → @ConfigurationProperties (inject one record everywhere)
D → @ConfigurationProperties + @Validated (fail-fast validation)
E → @Value (quick, test-only)

hints: @ConfigurationProperties shines with 3+ related properties
@Validated only works on @ConfigurationProperties classes
@Value is fine for one-off properties

exercises.title: Complete a @ConfigurationProperties record
exercises.scenario: Property prefix and record fields must align.
exercises.instructions: Fill in blanks to bind `sms.gateway.*` properties to a record.

blanks.hint: Annotation that binds property groups to Java classes

hints: @ConfigurationProperties(prefix='sms.gateway'): binds all sms.gateway.* props
Relaxed binding: api-key → apiKey, rate-limit → rateLimit

exercises.title: Simple @ConfigurationProperties record
exercises.scenario: Group related properties into a typed record.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `AppProps` @ConfigurationProperties record that binds:
- `app.name` → String
- `app.version` → String
- `app.max-requests-per-minute` → int
- `app.debug-mode` → boolean

Also write the matching application.properties entries.
hints: Prefix 'app' matches all app.* properties
max-requests-per-minute → maxRequestsPerMinute (relaxed binding)
debug-mode → debugMode

exercises.title: Enable @ConfigurationProperties on Application class
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete Spring Boot Application class that:
1. Has @ConfigurationPropertiesScan to enable all @ConfigurationProperties
2. Has @EnableAsync and @EnableCaching for completeness
3. Declare AppProps and PaymentGatewayProps records (show the structure)
4. Show injection into a @Service via constructor
hints: @ConfigurationPropertiesScan: one annotation discovers all @ConfigurationProperties in the package
Alternative: @EnableConfigurationProperties(AppProps.class, PaymentGatewayProps.class)
Record is injected like any Spring bean via constructor

exercises.title: @Validated constraints on @ConfigurationProperties
exercises.scenario: Validation at startup prevents running with bad config.
exercises.instructions:
Write `PaymentGatewayProps` with @Validated and constraints:
- url: @NotBlank + @Pattern (must start with https://)
- timeoutMs: @Min(100) @Max(30000)
- maxRetries: @Min(1) @Max(5)
- apiKey: @NotBlank

Write one application.properties entry that would FAIL validation and explain why.
hints: @Validated on the class: triggers Bean Validation at startup
@Pattern(regexp='https://.*'): URL must start with https://
@NotBlank fails on empty string ('') as well as null

exercises.title: Dev and prod profile property files
exercises.scenario: Different environments need different property values.
exercises.instructions:
Write three property files for a payment service:
1. `application.properties` — shared: app name, port 8080, retry count 3
2. `application-dev.properties` — dev: H2 DB, sandbox API URL, DEBUG logging
3. `application-prod.properties` — prod: env var DB URL, live API URL, WARN logging

Use proper Spring Boot property names.
exercises.expected_solution:
# application.properties:
spring.application.name=payment-service
server.port=8080
payment.gateway.max-retries=3
payment.gateway.timeout-ms=5000

# application-dev.properties:
spring.datasource.url=jdbc:h2:mem:paymentdb
spring.datasource.driver-class-name=org.h2.Driver
spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=create-drop
spring.jpa.show-sql=true
payment.gateway.url=https://sandbox.razorpay.com
payment.gateway.api-key=rzp_test_sandbox123
logging.level.root=DEBUG

# application-prod.properties:
spring.datasource.url=${PROD_DB_URL}
spring.datasource.username=${PROD_DB_USER}
spring.datasource.password=${PROD_DB_PASSWORD}
spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=validate
payment.gateway.url=https://api.razorpay.com
payment.gateway.api-key=${RAZORPAY_LIVE_KEY}
logging.level.root=WARN
hints: Shared properties go in application.properties
Profile files override the shared properties
${ENV_VAR}: reads from environment variable

exercises.title: @Profile beans — dev mock vs prod real
exercises.scenario: @Profile controls which implementation is loaded per environment.
exercises.instructions:
Write:
1. `EmailService` interface with `send(String to, String body)` 
2. `MockEmailService` @Profile({'dev','test'}) — prints to console
3. `SmtpEmailService` @Profile('prod') — would connect to real SMTP
4. @SpringBootTest @ActiveProfiles('dev') that verifies MockEmailService is injected
hints: @Profile({'dev','test'}): active in either profile
@ActiveProfiles('dev'): test runs with dev profile
assertInstanceOf: checks the concrete type of the injected bean

exercises.title: Test @ConfigurationProperties loading
exercises.scenario: Verify properties are correctly bound with @SpringBootTest.
exercises.instructions:
Write @SpringBootTest @ActiveProfiles('dev') tests that:
1. Verify PaymentGatewayProps.url() equals 'https://sandbox.razorpay.com'
2. Verify PaymentGatewayProps.maxRetries() equals 3
3. Verify AppProps.name() is not null or blank
4. Verify AppProps.debugMode() is true (in dev)
hints: @ActiveProfiles('dev'): loads application-dev.properties
@Autowired on @ConfigurationProperties record: Spring injects it like any bean
All 4 tests verify property values match the dev profile

exercises.title: Nested @ConfigurationProperties record
exercises.instructions:
Write a `NotificationProps` @ConfigurationProperties record with nested Email and Sms records. Bind:
- notification.enabled (boolean)
- notification.email.host, .port, .from, .tls-enabled
- notification.sms.url, .api-key, .max-per-day

Write the application.properties entries and show injection into a service.
exercises.starter_code:
import org.springframework.boot.context.properties.ConfigurationProperties;
// NotificationProps with nested Email and Sms records
hints: Nested record: fields bind to notification.email.* sub-prefix
Record within record: same relaxed binding rules (tls-enabled → tlsEnabled)
props.email().host(): access nested record fields via accessor methods

exercises.title: Profile property override order
exercises.instructions:
Given these files, what is the effective value of each property when `spring.profiles.active=dev`?

```properties
# application.properties:
server.port=8080
logging.level.root=INFO
payment.gateway.retries=3
payment.gateway.url=NOT_SET

# application-dev.properties:
logging.level.root=DEBUG
payment.gateway.url=https://sandbox.razorpay.com
```
exercises.expected_solution:
server.port = 8080 (from application.properties, not overridden)
logging.level.root = DEBUG (overridden by application-dev.properties)
payment.gateway.retries = 3 (from application.properties, not overridden)
payment.gateway.url = https://sandbox.razorpay.com (overridden by dev profile)

test_cases.expected:
server.port = 8080 (from application.properties, not overridden)
logging.level.root = DEBUG (overridden by application-dev.properties)
payment.gateway.retries = 3 (from application.properties, not overridden)
payment.gateway.url = https://sandbox.razorpay.com (overridden by dev profile)

hints: application.properties always loads
application-dev.properties overlays on top, overriding matching keys
Properties not in the profile file keep their base values

exercises.title: Zerodha-style trading config with profiles
exercises.scenario: Dev uses NSE sandbox; prod uses live NSE feed.
exercises.instructions:
Build a complete configuration setup for a trading service:
- `TradingProps` @ConfigurationProperties: marketFeedUrl, maxOrdersPerMinute, tradingHoursStart, tradingHoursEnd
- `DatabaseProps`: url, username, password, poolSize
- application.properties: shared config
- application-dev.properties: sandbox, H2, small pool
- application-prod.properties: env var DB, live NSE, larger pool
- @SpringBootTest @ActiveProfiles('dev') with 3 assertions
exercises.starter_code:
// Complete Zerodha-style trading config
// TradingProps, DatabaseProps, 3 property files, @SpringBootTest
hints: Two separate @ConfigurationProperties records for different prefixes
@ActiveProfiles('dev'): loads application-dev.properties overlay
db.url().contains('h2'): H2 URL check without hardcoding

exercises.title: @Profile conditional bean with CommandLineRunner
exercises.scenario: @Profile('dev') beans seed test data only in dev.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `DevDataSeeder` @Component @Profile('dev') that:
1. Implements CommandLineRunner
2. Injects ProductRepository
3. Seeds 3 test products on startup
4. Prints '[DEV] Seeded X products'

Also write a @SpringBootTest @ActiveProfiles('dev') test verifying seeder ran.
hints: @Profile('dev'): DevDataSeeder only runs when dev profile is active
CommandLineRunner.run() called after all beans are created
@ActiveProfiles('dev') in test: triggers the @Profile('dev') seeder

exercises.title: Validated config that fails startup on bad values
exercises.instructions:
Write a test that verifies your @ConfigurationProperties validation works:
1. Create a @SpringBootTest that overrides a required property with an empty string
2. Assert that the application context FAILS to load (ApplicationContextException)
3. Use `assertThrows` with the `SpringApplication.run()` or test context failure

Alternatively: demonstrate the validation works by showing a test that SUCCEEDS with valid properties and explain what would fail.
hints: @TestPropertySource(properties={...}): override specific properties in test
Context fails to start with @Validated + missing required property
Demonstrate with valid properties first, explain the failure scenario

exercises.title: Feature flag system with profiles
exercises.scenario: Profiles control which experimental features are active.
exercises.instructions:
Build a feature flag system:
- `FeatureFlags` @ConfigurationProperties: enableNewCheckout (boolean), enableMlRecommendations (boolean), enableBetaSearch (boolean)
- application-dev.properties: all flags = true (dev tries everything)
- application-prod.properties: only enableNewCheckout = true (prod cautious)
- `FeatureFlagService` that checks flags and routes accordingly
- @SpringBootTest test comparing flag values between dev and prod profiles
hints: Two separate @SpringBootTest classes with different @ActiveProfiles
enable-new-checkout → enableNewCheckout (relaxed binding)
Feature flag pattern: check the flag, return different routes/implementations

exercises.title: Complete payment service config — Module 14.4 synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete configuration for a Razorpay-style payment service:
1. `PaymentProps` record: gateway (url, timeoutMs, maxRetries, apiKey @NotBlank), webhook (url, secret @NotBlank), limits (maxAmountInPaise @Max(10000000), minAmountInPaise @Min(100))
2. Three property files: shared, dev, prod
3. @Profile('dev') DevPaymentSeeder, @Profile('prod') LivePaymentMonitor
4. @SpringBootTest dev profile: 4 assertions on props
5. @SpringBootTest prod profile: verify different url

All tests pass.
hints: @Valid on nested record fields: propagates @Validated to nested records
Two @SpringBootTest classes, each with a different @ActiveProfiles
props.gateway().url(): access nested record through the outer record
